questions.stem: A 25-year-old woman presents with acute right eye pain after being scratched by her toddler's fingernail. She reports photophobia and tearing. On examination, visual acuity is 6/9. Fluorescein staining reveals a linear epithelial defect with no infiltrate. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The linear epithelial defect after a scratch, with no infiltrate, is classic for corneal abrasion. Corneal ulcer typically has an infiltrate and often a more severe presentation. Herpes simplex keratitis usually has a dendritic pattern. Foreign body would show an object or rust ring. Acute glaucoma presents with elevated IOP and cloudy cornea.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old soft contact lens wearer presents with a gritty sensation in his right eye for 2 days. He admits to sleeping in his lenses. On examination, visual acuity is 6/12. There is a small epithelial defect with no infiltrate on fluorescein staining. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Contact lens overwear can cause an epithelial defect without infection. The absence of infiltrate and hypopyon makes keratitis unlikely. Recurrent erosion usually has a history of prior abrasion. The clinical picture is most consistent with simple corneal abrasion.

questions.stem: A 3-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department by his mother. He is crying and photophobic. The mother says he poked his eye with a toy. Examination is difficult but fluorescein staining reveals a small, irregular epithelial defect. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: History of trauma with a positive stain is diagnostic of corneal abrasion. Conjunctivitis is bilateral with discharge. Corneal ulcer would have infiltrate and is rare in children. Foreign body would be visible. Congenital glaucoma presents with buphthalmos and photophobia but not staining defect.

questions.stem: A 20-year-old woman with seasonal allergic rhinitis presents with acute left eye pain after rubbing her eye vigorously. She has mild conjunctival injection. Fluorescein staining shows multiple tiny epithelial defects over the cornea. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Vigorous eye rubbing can cause superficial punctate abrasions. The staining pattern of multiple tiny defects is typical. Allergic conjunctivitis is bilateral and itching predominant. Corneal ulcer would have infiltrate. Herpes simplex is dendritic. Dry eye gives punctate staining but not from trauma.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with chronic blepharitis presents with right eye discomfort and redness. He has noticed that his lower eyelid turns inward. On examination, there is a linear epithelial defect on the inferior cornea with fluorescein uptake. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The in-turning of the eyelid (entropion) causes trichiasis, leading to mechanical corneal abrasion. The linear defect on the inferior cornea is typical. Exposure keratopathy is more diffuse and associated with lagophthalmos. Neurotrophic keratitis is often central and painless. This is a corneal abrasion from the lashes.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a painful, red left eye after being poked by a child's finger. He reports photophobia and excessive tearing. Visual acuity is 6/9 in the affected eye. Which single investigation is most appropriate to confirm the diagnosis of corneal abrasion?
options: Fluorescein staining and slit lamp examination
questions.explanation: Fluorescein dye binds to exposed basement membrane of the corneal epithelium, highlighting the defect under cobalt blue light. This is the gold standard test.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old contact lens wearer presents with a red, painful left eye for 24 hours. He removed the lens last night. Slit lamp examination reveals a small corneal epithelial defect with mild surrounding oedema but no visible infiltrate. Which investigation is most appropriate to exclude microbial keratitis?
options: Corneal scrape for Gram stain and culture
questions.explanation: In contact lens wearers with a corneal epithelial defect, the main risk is microbial keratitis. A corneal scrape for microbiology is the gold standard to confirm or exclude infection. Fluorescein staining only confirms the epithelial defect, not infection.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with a 3-day history of a painful, gritty feeling in her right eye that started upon waking. She recalls a similar episode two months ago after a fingernail scratch. Examination shows a ridge of loose epithelium at the site of the previous abrasion. Which investigation is most appropriate to confirm the diagnosis of recurrent corneal erosion?
options: Fluorescein staining and slit lamp examination after several blinks
questions.explanation: In recurrent corneal erosion, the epithelium is loosely adherent. Multiple blinks can cause the loose epithelium to detach, and fluorescein staining will reveal the epithelial defect. This is the first-line diagnostic test.

questions.stem: A 5-year-old boy is brought to the paediatric emergency department with a painful right eye after being hit by a toy. He is crying and refuses to open his eyes for slit lamp examination. Which approach is most appropriate to confirm the diagnosis of corneal abrasion?
options: Apply fluorescein drops and examine with a Wood's lamp
Perform a fluorescein strip and wait for his cooperation
Attempt forced lid opening with a speculum under topical anaesthesia
Instil topical anaesthetic and attempt slit lamp examination again
questions.explanation: A Wood's lamp with a cobalt blue filter is portable and less intimidating for children; if a corneal abrasion is present, the fluorescein will fluoresce. This is a practical alternative when slit lamp examination is not tolerated.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old welder presents after a corneal foreign body was removed. Slit lamp examination with fluorescein reveals a central area of non-staining surrounded by a ring of staining. What is the most likely explanation for this finding?
options: The patient has a simple corneal abrasion
There is a retained intra-stromal rust ring
There is a developing corneal ulcer
The foreign body was completely removed
questions.explanation: A rust ring from a metallic foreign body does not stain with fluorescein, but the surrounding epithelial defect does. This produces a characteristic central non-staining area with a ring of staining.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old office worker presents with a painful, gritty right eye after a foreign body sensation earlier today. Slit-lamp examination reveals a small, linear corneal abrasion with no infiltrate or hypopyon. There is no history of contact lens use. He has no known allergies. What is the single most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: Oral aciclovir 400 mg five times daily
Topical chloramphenicol 0.5% eye drops four times daily for 5 days
Topical dexamethasone 0.1% eye drops four times daily
questions.explanation: For a simple, uncomplicated corneal abrasion in a non-contact lens wearer, topical chloramphenicol is the first-line prophylactic antibiotic to prevent infection. Steroids are contraindicated as they increase infection risk. Patching is no longer recommended. Oral antivirals are for herpetic keratitis, not indicated here.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old contact lens wearer presents with a painful red eye and photophobia after sleeping in her lenses. Slit-lamp examination reveals a central corneal abrasion with a surrounding infiltrate and a small hypopyon. What is the single most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: Topical aciclovir ointment five times daily
questions.explanation: Contact lens-related corneal abrasions are high risk for Pseudomonas aeruginosa infection. A topical fluoroquinolone (e.g., ofloxacin) provides broad-spectrum cover including Pseudomonas. Chloramphenicol does not cover Pseudomonas. Patching is contraindicated. Oral antibiotics are not first-line. Aciclovir treats herpes, not bacterial keratitis.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man presents with severe eye pain, photophobia, and blurred vision after being poked in the eye by a tree branch. Slit-lamp examination shows a large corneal abrasion (approximately 40% of corneal surface) with marked ciliary injection. He rates pain 9/10. What is the single most appropriate pharmacological therapy to relieve his pain?
options: Topical prednisolone 1% eye drops four times daily
Topical tetracaine 0.5% eye drops as needed
Oral ibuprofen 400 mg three times daily
questions.explanation: A large corneal abrasion causes ciliary spasm, which is a major source of pain. A single dose of a short-acting cycloplegic (e.g., cyclopentolate) relieves this spasm effectively. Topical anaesthetics (tetracaine) are never prescribed for home use due to risk of corneal toxicity and delayed healing. Oral analgesia (ibuprofen, paracetamol) is adjunctive but not the most specific therapy. Steroids are contraindicated acutely.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman presents with recurrent episodes of eye pain and photophobia upon waking for the past month. She has a history of a fingernail scratch to the cornea 6 months ago. Slit-lamp examination reveals a small area of loose epithelium with negative fluorescein staining but epithelial irregularity. What is the single most appropriate first-line prophylactic treatment?
options: Topical chloramphenicol ointment at night for 2 weeks
Lubricating eye ointment (e.g., paraffin-based) at bedtime
Corneal debridement with a sterile needle
questions.explanation: This is recurrent corneal erosion syndrome. First-line management is conservative: nocturnal lubrication with a paraffin-based ointment to reduce epithelial friction during sleep. Topical antibiotics are not indicated unless infection is present. Bandage contact lenses or debridement are reserved for refractory cases. Cyclopentolate is for acute pain, not prophylaxis.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old pregnant woman (16 weeks gestation) presents with a painful right eye after a piece of dust got in it. Slit-lamp examination reveals a small corneal abrasion. She has a known allergy to chloramphenicol (urticaria). What is the single most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: Topical fusidic acid 1% gel twice daily
Topical erythromycin 0.5% eye ointment four times daily
Topical prednisolone 1% eye drops twice daily
Topical ofloxacin 0.3% eye drops four times daily
questions.explanation: In pregnancy, chloramphenicol is generally safe topically but is contraindicated here due to allergy. Erythromycin is a safe alternative with good Gram-positive cover for prophylaxis. Fluoroquinolones (ofloxacin) are generally avoided in pregnancy due to arthropathy concerns. Fusidic acid has poor Gram-negative cover and is not first-line. Steroids are contraindicated. Observation alone is not standard of care for an abrasion.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a 2-hour history of a painful, red left eye after being poked in the eye by a tree branch. Visual acuity is 6/9 in the affected eye. Slit-lamp examination shows a linear corneal abrasion with no infiltrate or hypopyon. Fluorescein staining is positive. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Refer to ophthalmology for same-day review.
Apply a chloramphenicol eye ointment and pad the eye shut.
Irrigate the eye with normal saline to remove any debris.
Arrange a CT head to rule out foreign body.
Prescribe topical ofloxacin drops four times daily.
questions.explanation: For a simple, uncomplicated corneal abrasion, the immediate management is to apply topical antibiotic (e.g., chloramphenicol ointment) and pad the eye closed to promote healing. Referral is not needed unless there is suspicion of infection, foreign body, or complications. Irrigation and CT are not indicated without evidence of debris or intraocular foreign body.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old contact lens wearer presents with a painful, red right eye for 12 hours, worse this morning. She reports photophobia and a gritty feeling. Slit-lamp exam reveals a small central corneal abrasion with a faint white infiltrate and minimal cell in the anterior chamber. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Apply a cycloplegic agent and prescribe topical antibiotics.
Start topical chloramphenicol drops and review in 24 hours.
Refer urgently to ophthalmology for same-day assessment.
Perform a corneal scrape for culture and sensitivity.
Remove the contact lens and prescribe lubricating drops.
questions.explanation: The presence of a corneal infiltrate in a contact lens wearer raises suspicion for microbial keratitis, an emergency requiring urgent ophthalmology review. Empiric treatment should be ophthalmology-led. Delaying referral risks sight-threatening infection.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with type 2 diabetes and a history of recurrent corneal abrasions presents with a painful left eye for 1 day. He has a small abrasion with surrounding epithelial irregularity and no signs of infection. He is on topical lubricants and has had two previous abrasions in the same eye. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Refer for corneal debridement under local anaesthetic.
Arrange a bandage contact lens fitting.
Apply topical chloramphenicol ointment and pad the eye.
Prescribe a course of oral doxycycline for 3 months.
questions.explanation: For an acute, uncomplicated abrasion in a patient with recurrent erosions, the immediate management is the same as a new abrasion: topical antibiotic and padding to prevent further trauma and infection. The other options (doxycycline, bandage lens, graft, debridement) are for management of recurrent erosion syndrome, not the acute episode.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old woman presents to the ED after being hit in the eye by a champagne cork. She has severe pain, photophobia, and blurred vision. Visual acuity is reduced to counting fingers. Slit-lamp examination reveals a large corneal abrasion with corneal oedema, a shallow anterior chamber, and irregular pupil. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Refer to ophthalmology as an outpatient tomorrow.
Pad the eye and prescribe topical chloramphenicol.
Perform a Seidel test to check for globe rupture.
Request an immediate CT head and orbits.
questions.explanation: Given the mechanism (high-velocity impact) and signs of possible globe rupture (shallow anterior chamber, irregular pupil), the priority is to perform a Seidel test to identify a leaking wound. Padding and outpatient follow-up are dangerous if there is an open globe.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old builder attends the ED with a painful gritty left eye after grinding metal without goggles. He has a linear corneal abrasion with fluorescein uptake and no visible foreign body on slit lamp. An X-ray of the orbits is normal. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Refer to ophthalmology for removal of intraocular foreign body.
Evert the upper eyelid to inspect for a subtarsal foreign body.
Prescribe topical anaesthetic drops for pain relief as needed.
Arrange a non-contrast CT of the orbits.
Apply a cycloplegic drop and topical antibiotic ointment.
questions.explanation: In a high-risk mechanism (grinding metal), a subtarsal foreign body is common but may be missed on slit lamp. Eversion of the upper lid is essential to identify and remove it. CT is not indicated if X-ray is normal and no suspicion of intraocular FB; topical anaesthetics should never be prescribed for home use.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old presents with a red, painful eye after being scratched by a cat. On examination, there is a 2 mm corneal abrasion that stains with fluorescein. The anterior chamber shows a small collection of pus inferiorly. Which finding most urgently requires ophthalmology referral?
questions.explanation: Hypopyon (pus in the anterior chamber) indicates severe intraocular infection (endophthalmitis or hypopyon keratitis), which is a vision-threatening emergency requiring immediate ophthalmology referral. Conjunctival injection and ciliary flush are common in corneal abrasion but not emergent; corneal infiltrate suggests infection but is less urgent than hypopyon; dendritic ulcer indicates herpes simplex keratitis, which is serious but not as immediately sight-threatening as hypopyon.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old contact lens wearer presents with a red, painful eye. Slit-lamp examination reveals a corneal epithelial defect with an underlying white opacity. Which clinical finding is most concerning for bacterial keratitis?
questions.explanation: Corneal infiltrate (a white opacity in the corneal stroma underlying an epithelial defect) is the hallmark of microbial keratitis in contact lens users, necessitating urgent referral for culture and intensive antibiotics. Hypopyon is a later sign; dendritic ulcer is characteristic of herpes simplex; fixed dilated pupil suggests acute glaucoma; chemosis is non-specific.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old with a history of recurrent eye pain and photophobia presents with a red eye. On fluorescein staining, a branching ulcer is seen. Which finding is diagnostic of herpes simplex keratitis?
questions.explanation: A dendritic (branching) ulcer on fluorescein staining is pathognomonic for herpes simplex keratitis. This pattern distinguishes it from a simple abrasion (linear or geographic) and requires antiviral treatment; topical steroids would worsen it. Hypopyon, infiltrate, fixed pupil, and neovascularization are not specific for HSV.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old contact lens wearer presents with a red, painful eye after swimming in a lake without removing lenses. On slit-lamp examination, there is a corneal abrasion and a ring-shaped infiltrate in the stroma. Which finding is most suggestive of Acanthamoeba keratitis?
questions.explanation: A ring infiltrate (peripheral or central) is a classic sign of Acanthamoeba keratitis, especially in contact lens wearers exposed to water. This is a vision-threatening infection that requires specific antimicrobial therapy. Hypopyon, non-ring infiltrate, dendritic ulcer, and fixed pupil are not characteristic.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old presents with sudden onset severe right eye pain, nausea, and blurred vision. On examination, the cornea is cloudy and the pupil is mid-dilated and fixed. Which finding indicates a diagnosis of acute angle closure glaucoma rather than corneal abrasion?
questions.explanation: A fixed, mid-dilated pupil in the setting of a painful red eye with cloudy cornea is a red flag for acute angle closure glaucoma, a medical emergency requiring immediate intraocular pressure reduction. This finding is not seen in simple corneal abrasion (where the pupil is normally reactive). Corneal edema is present but not specific; hypopyon, dendritic ulcer, and infiltrate are not present.
